summary: I was born and raised in Burma in 1987, but my life there was very difficult because my father was a well-known Karen activist and peacemaker. I had to hide my true identity and live under a different name to avoid being captured or tortured by the Burmese military. After graduating with a social work degree, the military discovered who I really was, and I had to escape from Burma to Thailand to save my life.

After fleeing Burma, I lived in a refugee camp in Thailand for several years. My father sent men to escort me safely across the border, and I was introduced to Dr. Cynthia Maung, a Karen doctor providing free healthcare to refugees along the Thai-Burma border. I volunteered as a midwife, nurse educator, and counselor at her clinic while preparing to resettle in the United States.

I came to the United States as a refugee in 2009, choosing it because it accepts many refugees from Burma. Minnesota became my new home, where I could reconnect with my culture and access resources to help me adjust. I studied social work at Metropolitan State University and later worked as a social worker in Ramsey County, advocating for the Karen community in the Twin Cities.

I pursued my education in social work at Metropolitan State University in St. Paul after arriving in the United States. This education helped me gain the skills needed to serve my community effectively. While studying there, I also worked toward becoming a licensed clinical social worker to better support Karen refugees and youth in Minnesota.

I worked as a social worker in Ramsey County, Minnesota, where I served Karen youth and refugees with their medical and mental health needs. This role allowed me to directly support my community in navigating challenges in a new country. Ramsey County became an important place for me to contribute and advocate for culturally appropriate services for the Karen population.

In 2014, I founded Karen Human Services of America, a nonprofit organization dedicated to serving the Karen community in the United States. I established this with seven board members to address the lack of culturally appropriate services for Karen refugees. This organization carries on the legacy of my father and my commitment to volunteerism and community empowerment.

When I arrived in Thailand, my father introduced me to Dr. Cynthia Maung, a Karen doctor who provides free healthcare to refugees along the Thai-Burma border. I volunteered at her clinic as a midwife, nurse educator, and counselor, gaining valuable experience while helping others in need. Dr. Maung's work inspired me to continue serving my community and pursue social work.
